full_response:
Let  

 φ(x₁,…,xₙ)        (1)  

be an arbitrary CNF formula.  
Our task is to decide whether φ is satisfiable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1.  A polynomial-time transformation that forces “evenness”.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce one fresh variable y and set  

 φ′(x₁,…,xₙ,y)  :=  φ(x₁,…,xₙ) ∧ (y ∨ ¬y).      (2)  

• (y ∨ ¬y) is a single CNF clause, so φ′ is a CNF formula, and the
  transformation (1) → (2) is clearly polynomial‐time.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Properties of the transformation.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Preservation of satisfiability  
 φ is satisfiable  ⇔  φ′ is satisfiable, because the extra clause
 (y ∨ ¬y) is a tautology.

2. Number of satisfying assignments  
 If φ has k satisfying assignments on the variables x₁,…,xₙ,
 then for every such assignment the new variable y may take the
 value 0 or 1 independently.  
 Hence
  #Sat(φ′) = 2k.  
 Therefore #Sat(φ′) is always even, including the case k = 0.

Consequently the image φ′ of every CNF formula φ belongs to the
promise set  

 EVEN-SAT = { ψ : ψ has 0,2,4,… satisfying assignments }.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Contradiction if an alleged polynomial-time algorithm exists.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Assume, toward contradiction, that there exists a deterministic
polynomial-time algorithm A that decides satisfiability correctly
on every formula that is promised to lie in EVEN-SAT.

Given an arbitrary CNF formula φ, compute φ′ by (2) and run A on φ′.
Because φ′ ∈ EVEN-SAT, the promise is satisfied, so A outputs  
 “Satisfiable” ⇔ φ′ is satisfiable ⇔ φ is satisfiable.

Thus we would have a polynomial-time algorithm for ordinary CNF-SAT,
which is NP-complete.  This would imply P = NP, contradicting the
assumption P ≠ NP.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conclusion
--------------------------------------------------------------------
No polynomial-time algorithm can decide CNF-SAT on the promise set
“formulas with an even number of satisfying assignments’’ unless
P = NP.

\boxed{\text{Impossible.  Even-SAT is as hard as general SAT; a
polynomial-time algorithm would yield P=NP.}}

Confidence: 91%
extracted_answer:
\text{Impossible.  Even-SAT is as hard as general SAT; a
polynomial-time algorithm would yield P=NP.}
